pull_requests.title: kubelet: lookup node address for external provider if none is set

nodes.bodyText:
/sig node
/assign @mtaufen @mcrute @cheftako

/sig cloudprovider
/area cloudprovider
/priority important-longterm
@liggitt do you see this being a problem for kubelet serving certificates with node addresses? (#65594)

@liggitt do you see this being a problem for kubelet serving certificates with node addresses?

the only issue I can think of would be if the serving certificate approver process refuses to approve the self-detected addresses. in that case, the kubelet could be waiting for approval for the old SANs for a long time (manager#rotateCerts() looks like it waits up to 15 minutes before giving up and re-requesting)
@mtaufen can I get a review from you please?
/assign @dchen1107
@dchen1107 @liggitt can I get a review from you please? This is going to unblock some bootstrapping issues we have with out-of-tree cloud providers
is the potential 15-minute delay before getting a serving certificate seen as problematic?

@liggitt I could be understanding the kubelet serving certificate code incorrectly so sorry in advance. Don't we detect changes against the template prior to the rotation deadline so if getAddresses returns a new set we should detect that and restart certificate rotation?

Ah I see what you mean now, the 15m wait time is from inside rotateCerts which would already be running against the old addresses and blocking. But wouldn't this be an existing problem for all nodes regardless of provider if the certificate request is not approved?

But wouldn't this be an existing problem for all nodes regardless of provider if the certificate request is not approved?

Yes, but when running with a cloud provider, we only request certs for addresses the provider gave us, so denials because the addresses were wrong shouldn't be an issue. This is introducing that behavior for external cloud providers (which will be all cloud providers on an infinite time scale).

But the fallback behavior before the provider assigns address is the same set of address you would get if you didn't set --cloud-provider at all (hostname + resolvable hostname for internal IP). For those addresses, it would be a concern if the requested cert was denied right? What's the expectation for denied certs there (i.e a kubelet with no cloud provider)?

But the fallback behavior before the provider assigns address is the same set of address you would get if you didn't set --cloud-provider at all (hostname + resolvable hostname for internal IP)

That was intended to be a fallback for cloud providers that never returned node addresses, not ones that returned no addresses at first, and then at some later point started returning addresses. Do you know of cloud providers with that behavior?

not ones that returned no addresses at first, and then at some later point started returning addresses. Do you know of cloud providers with that behavior?

From my experience, every cloud provider implements node addresses.

That was intended to be a fallback for cloud providers that never returned node addresses

But the behavior of using hostname + resolvable hostname for internal IP only applies if you don't set --cloud-provider at all. So if a cloud provider didn't implement node addresses it wouldn't have these either. The behavior of auto discovering a node's address is a feature only for kubelet with no cloud provider as far as I can tell. So any baremetal cluster using TLS bootstrapping would run into the 15m blocking issue mentioned before. Am I missing something?
opened #77936 to short-circuit the 15-minute wait if the desired node addresses get modified
#77936 merged, this looks reasonable to me now. will defer to kubelet reviewers/approvers
/milestone v1.15
Hi! Friendly reminder from your release team: we are starting the code freeze for 1.15 tomorrow EOD. Looks like we have recent motion here. Can I get a thumbs up that this is still planned for 1.15?
@timmycarr thanks for the follow-up! Yes, this is still planned for v1.15, waiting for a kubelet approver for this PR though.
Any other kubelet approver available to look at this? cc @dchen1107 @derekwaynecarr @Random-Liu @vishh @yujuhong
/milestone v1.16

/uncc
this still looks good to me, will leave to @kubernetes/sig-node-pr-reviews for ack
/assign
@sjenning can you take a look?  we run a self-hosted control plane as well so would like your review.
is there a pointer i can reference for how external cloud provider with self hosted control planes are recommended for bootstrapping?

is there a pointer i can reference for how external cloud provider with self hosted control planes are recommended for bootstrapping?

I've only tried it with kubeadm, but as far as I can tell there isn't much on this because it  doesn't work. The self-hosted components rely on the kubelet/node for the API server's advertise address which relies on the node object having an InternalIP or ExternalIP set. For internal cloud provider this works fine because it queries instance metadata for node addresses, for the external case it won't get node addresses until the external provider is running which it can't without the control plane up. cc @neolit123 @fabriziopandini

If you know the API server advertise address ahead of time I think you can work around it but it kind of defeats the purpose of self-hosting if you need to manually resync that value rather than relying on what Kubernetes says is the node's address.

/hold
we need to ensure that we have a clear bootstrapping flow identified before proceeding on this imo.
is there a kep that we can iterate or extend upon for this as part of overall external cloud provider transition?

we need to ensure that we have a clear bootstrapping flow identified

fwiw I think the bootstraping flow is well identified for the internal cloud provider case already, it just breaks for the external case because of it's assumptions around node addresses. Will let kubeadm maintainers comment on this one though.

is there a kep that we can iterate or extend upon for this as part of overall external cloud provider transition?

We have a KEP but it does not address self-hosting since it's still considered an alpha feature and wasn't part of the initial work on external providers.

@andrewsykim given that there are conformant k8s distributions that run self-hosted, i think we need to capture a recommendation on how those distributions should proceed.  i am happy to help rally resources to work through this topic if its not yet defined, but i think we need to account for it as part of the transition.

@derekwaynecarr that's good to know, thank you! I'm curious if conformance actually covers the bootstrapping of self-hosted clusters also. I assume it only covers post-bootstrap which this PR shouldn't change - either way happy to dig into this if needed

hi @andrewsykim ,

This should alleviate some of the common bootstrapping problems we see with external providers because the kubelet hosting the control plane does not have node addresses set. kubeadm self-hosting is the first example that comes to mind.

if by self-hosting we mean running the kubeadm created control-plane as DaemonSets (and not the popular internet meaning which is running static-pods), then sadly this feature is pretty much unsupported at this point in kubeadm. it remained in alpha for a long time due to a set of major caveats:
https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/production-environment/tools/kubeadm/self-hosting/#caveats
reading back, the conclusion was that self-hosting and external CPs will not work in kubeadm:
ref: #60607 (comment)

I've only tried it with kubeadm, but as far as I can tell there isn't much on this because it doesn't work. The self-hosted components rely on the kubelet/node for the API server's advertise address which relies on the node object having an InternalIP or ExternalIP set.

given my comment above, perhaps kubeadm is not the right tool to base evaluations of this change upon.

For internal cloud provider this works fine because it queries instance metadata for node addresses, for the external case it won't get node addresses until the external provider is running which it can't without the control plane up

i might be lacking context on the external CP case.
so once the control plane is up the external CP can provide a node address at which point the control plane has to be restarted? does that mean that a new api server serving certificate has to be recreated to include a new advertise address?

I'm curious if conformance actually covers the bootstrapping of self-hosted clusters

kubeadm does not have e2e tests for self-hosting at this point. i remember seeing self-hosting tests in the suite, but i don't think these are part of conformance.
i am particularly concerned about using static pods with external cloud controller manager.  is there a concern with that approach?

if by self-hosting we mean running the kubeadm created control-plane as DaemonSets (and not the popular internet meaning which is running static-pods), then sadly this feature is pretty much unsupported at this point in kubeadm.

Apologies if this wasn't clear - yes I was only referring to kubeadm self-hosting where the control plane is bootstrapped into a DaemonSet. Static Pods with external cloud providers works and is adopted.
@andrewsykim thanks for clarifying.  is there a document/kep that describes how a static pod approach is enabled?

This PR was specifically open to address the kubeadm self-hosting case, but it also addresses general trouble shooting issues for the external CP case. More specifically, if a node fails to register with an external cloud provider, it's generally hard to trouble shoot pods running on cluster cause you can't fetch any logs without node addresses being set. With this change, node's have more functionality prior to registration with the cloud provider.

@andrewsykim thanks for clarifying. is there a document/kep that describes how a static pod approach is enabled?

It's not any different from how you would run static pods previously aside from changing some flags (mainly --cloud-provider=external) which we document here https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/running-cloud-controller. And the external add-on for the cloud provider should be documented by the cloud provider.

/hold cancel
apologies for initial confusion.  this makes sense.
/approved
/lgtm
/approve

commit.message:
kubelet: lookup node address for external provider if none is set

Signed-off-by: Andrew Sy Kim <kiman@vmware.com>

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind feature
What this PR does / why we need it:
This changes the node address lookup in the kubelet for external providers. If --cloud-provider=external and the kubelet has no node address set already, we attempt to fill its node address with what we can find at runtime (hostname + internal IP). In most cloud providers, whatever hostname/internal IP the kubelet sets at startup will get overwritten later.
This should alleviate some of the common bootstrapping problems we see with external providers because the kubelet hosting the control plane does not have node addresses set.
Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
Special notes for your reviewer:
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?:
Attempt to set the kubelet's hostname & internal IP if `--cloud-provider=external` and no node addresses exists

commit.message: Interrupt WaitForCertificate if desired kubelet serving cert changes

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind cleanup
What this PR does / why we need it:
If the desired serving certificate for a kubelet changes while it is waiting for approval on a previously requested certificate, it can block for up to 15 minutes before making a new request for the new serving cert.
This PR threads a context into WaitForCertificate so we can cancel the request if a changed cert template is detected.
Relevant to #75229 (comment)
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?:
NONE

/cc @mikedanese @awly @andrewsykim
/sig node auth

commit.message:
DRA: support non-pod references in ReservedFor

Signed-off-by: MohammedSaalif <salifud2004@gmail.com>
Expand reservation validation comment in syncClaim as suggested by mortent
Address feedback: rename valid to remaining and remove obsolete TODO

pull_requests.bodyText:
Closing previous PR #136430 due to a local environment error that caused a massive accidental force-push. This new PR contains the clean, targeted fix and addresses the feedback from @troychiu.
Problem: TheResourceClaim controller would fail to process and clean up stale pod references if it encountered any non-pod references in the status.reservedFor field. This halted the controller's operation, preventing necessary cleanup.
Solution: Modified the syncClaim function to gracefully handle unknown (non-pod) ReservedFor entries. Instead of returning an error, it now logs a message and continues processing, ensuring that valid pod references can still be cleaned up.
Changes:
Updated pkg/controller/resourceclaim/controller.go to keep unknown references and continue processing.
Integrated a regression test case into the TestSyncHandler suite in pkg/controller/resourceclaim/controller_test.go
using realistic test data (testPodWithResource).
/kind feature
Added support for unknown (non-pod) references in ResourceClaim status.reservedFor. The controller now gracefully ignores these entries instead of returning an error, ensuring that stale pod references can still be cleaned up.
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
The ResourceClaim controller now correctly handles unknown (non-pod) references in the status.reservedFor field by skipping them instead of halting the sync process.

commit.message: feat: Add helper function for client-go to compare resource version
feat: Add conformance tests for all resources for comparable resource version
Add additional types for resource version comparison testing
feat: Add matcher and conformance tests ensuring that RV is uint128
Document 0 as a special case in RV comparison

/kind feature

commit.message:
feat(ccm): watch based route controller

Introduces a new watch based route controller for the cloud-controller-manager. This prevents the exhaustion of API rate limits through an overagressive routes controller. This is further described in #60646. The corresponding feature gate is called "CloudControllerManagerWatchBasedRoutesReconciliation".
fix: check error of AddEventHandler
fix: spelling
fix: missing update of feature gates
feat: update routes on resync
feat: minimal route resync period interval
fix: rate limit route reconciles
fix: feature gate min version
feat: add feature gate to kube features
docs: cleanup docstrings

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind feature
What this PR does / why we need it:
The route controller in the cloud-controller-manager currently operates with a static reconciliation interval. Depending on the scale of the cluster, this can quickly lead to exceeding the rate limits set by cloud providers.
This PR introduces a new watch-based route controller that triggers reconciliation only when a node is added, deleted, or when there are updates to the Status.Addresses or PodCIDR fields.
Additionally, a new feature gate, CloudControllerManagerWatchBasedRoutesReconciliation, is introduced, which is disabled by default.
Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
Fixes #60646
Special notes for your reviewer:
KEP 5237 is merged and has status implementable.
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
Add cloud-controller-manager feature gate CloudControllerManagerWatchBasedRoutesReconciliation

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:
Implementation of KEP 5237

commit.message: chore: change error_matcher.go to use test interface instead of importing testing pkg
feat: add declarative validation metrics and associated runtime verification tests

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind feature

What this PR does / why we need it:
Adds runtime verification testing for Declarative Validation as outlined in the KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/5073-declarative-validation-with-validation-gen/README.md#runtime-verification-testing
Introduces new metrics for declarative validation in staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/validation/metrics.go:

declarative_validation_mismatch_total
declarative_validation_panic_total

When DeclarativeValidation=enabled, the runtime verification tests checks that any validation errors from the imperative (hand-written) rules matches those generated by the declarative rules.  In the case that the errors do not match as expected, these runtime verification checks log + emit a metric declarative_validation_mismatch_total to notify of the mismatch.  This means that during normal operation, the declarative_validation_mismatch_total metric should be zero. Additionally the new ValidateDeclaratively method is also robust to panics (recovers) as an extra precaution.  If this method panics, control will be recovered and logging + a new metric -
declarative_validation_panic_total will be emitted.  This means that during normal operation, the declarative_validation_panic_total metric should also be zero.
Feature Gate Behavior Matrix:



Feature Gates
DeclarativeValidation=disabled
DeclarativeValidation=enabled (DEFAULT)




DeclarativeValidationTakeover=disabled
• Only imperative validation runs• No comparison is done ( and thus no metrics emitted)
• Both imperative and declarative validation run• Validation errors compared, mismatches logged• Metrics emitted for mismatches/panics• Imperative validation errors are authoritative and returned to users


DeclarativeValidationTakeover=enabled (DEFAULT)
• Only imperative validation runs• Takeover has no effect when base feature is disabled
• Both imperative and declarative validation run• Validation errors compared, mismatches logged• Metrics emitted for mismatches/panics• For fields where there is Declarative Validation defined, Declarative Validation errors are authoritative and returned to users (if there is no Declarative Validation alternative for a field, Imperative validation are authoratiative and returned)



Which issue(s) this PR fixes:

It is part of kubernetes/enhancements#5073
Special notes for your reviewer:
PR based on feature branch PR here - jpbetz#124
The first commit - 3dfe33b is to resolve a hack/verify-testing-import.sh test error around error_matcher.go.  This is likely less prioritized for review as it is a small refactoring of the error_matcher.go.
The second commit - 75b9194 contains the logic related to the runtime verification tests and new metrics.  This is higher priority for review.
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

NONE

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

- [KEP]: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/5073-declarative-validation-with-validation-gen/README.md

commit.message:
Enable validation-gen on core/v1

This enables generation but does not activate the strategy calling of
declarative.

ReplicationController: Enable declarative validation

After declarative validation is enabled in the ReplicationController
strategy in this way, the generated declarative validation code
in pkg/apis/core/v1/zz.generated.validations.go will be run
when the strategy validates ReplicationController.

Co-authored-by: Tim Hockin <thockin@google.com>
Co-authored-by: Aaron Prindle <aprindle@google.com>
Co-authored-by: Yongrui Lin <yongrlin@google.com>
Co-authored-by: David Eads <deads@redhat.com>

ReplicationController: Add declarative validation test suite

Introduce a test suite that ensures declarative test cases
are fully tested and that validation errors are compared
with handwritten validation to ensure consistency.

Co-authored-by: Tim Hockin <thockin@google.com>
Co-authored-by: Aaron Prindle <aprindle@google.com>
Co-authored-by: Yongrui Lin <yongrlin@google.com>
Enable versioned validation fuzz testing for core v1 group

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind feature
What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR:


Enables validation-gen for core v1 group: +k8s:validation-gen tags in pkg/apis/core/v1/doc.go enable generation for the core v1 API group. When enabled validation-gen parses "+" tags i staging/src/k8s.io/api/core/v1/types.go and generates validation code into pkg/apis/core/v1/zz_generated.validations.go.


Updates ReplicationController strategy to run generated declarative validation: When the DeclarativeValidation gate is enabled, the strategies' validate functions will execute both the existing hand-written validation logic and the newly generated declarative validation:

The validation errors returned by both methods are compared.
If the errors do not match for validations that are intended to be migrated to declarative, a metric named declarative_validation_mismatch_total is incremented.
If the declarative validation code encounters a panic, a metric named declarative_validation_panic_total is incremented.
Validation errors reported back to the caller respect the DeclarativeValidationTakeover feature gate:

For validation rules that have been migrated to declarative: When DeclarativeValidationTakeover is enabled, validation errors produced by declarative validation are returned. Otherwise, hand-written validation errors are returned. If declarative validation panics, an additional internal error is returned to report the problem.
For validation rules that have not yet been migrated, the existing hand-written validation errors will continue to be returned.





Enables declarative validation testing: Declarative validation is performed on API versions (not on the internal version like hand written validation). Fuzz testing is enabled for the core v1 group's validation.  Validation tests in declarative_validation_test.go are updated to use apitesting.VerifyVersionedValidationEquivalence to validate the (declarative) versioned validation.


Does NOT migrate any validations: Validation migrations are introduced in a subsequent PR:

#130725



Special notes for your reviewer:
This PR is currently stacked on #130705.  Please ignore the commits from that PR when reviewing this PR.
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
NONE

/assign @aroradaman @aojea

Reworks some code pertaining to masquerading in nftables kube-proxy, in particular to get rid of a bunch of jumps to avoid performance problems. (See #135639 (comment).)
In particular, it gets rid of the mark-for-masquerade chain, and instead just inlines the code that used to be there.

I'm curious if you've tested this to see if memory/cpu usage of nft is lower after the change
(although, I'd be surprised if it wasn't)

I didn't... also, note that the CPU improvements from this PR would be on the kernel side, not the userspace side. (ie, it helps to avoid the softlock problem from #135639)
I should probably remove the "memory" part because that was more associated with the "part 2" that I removed (eliminating per-endpoint chains).

Yes. The story behind this is #92035. When forwarding a packet through a VXLAN tunnel, the kernel reuses the skbuff, and in particular, does not clear the sk_mark, meaning we end up masquerading the packet a second time, which mostly has no effect because the VXLAN packet already has the node IP as its source IP anyway, but it does in some cases change the source port. There was a bug in older kernels where this would then result in it sending out the VXLAN packet with the wrong checksum. This was fixed a long time ago, but we kept the workaround in kube-proxy anyway because not everybody had a new-enough kernel. But (as the commit message notes), that argument doesn't apply to the nftables backend, where we require a newer kernel than that anyway.

/hold
hm... I guess I was arguing in #92035 that it was good to avoid the double-NAT anyway...
/retest-required

commit.message:
Simplify nftables masquerade rule

We originally made it multiple rules, matching iptables, but nftables
lets you do it all in a single rule.

Rework masquerade handling in nftables tracer

Process the actual rules rather than treating
"jump mark-for-masquerade" as having special semantics.

Remove mark-for-masquerade chain from nftables kube-proxy

Current kernels have problems with very large rulesets with lots of
jumps (because of inefficiency in the code that ensures the jumps
don't create loops). Get rid of a bunch of jumps (1 per endpoint and
usually 1 or 2 per service) by just doing mark-for-masquerade inline
rather than calling a helper chain to do it.

pull_requests.bodyText:
What this PR does / why we need it:
Reworks some code pertaining to masquerading in nftables kube-proxy, in particular to get rid of a bunch of jumps to avoid performance problems. (See #135639 (comment).)
In particular, it gets rid of the mark-for-masquerade chain, and instead just inlines the code that used to be there.
(An earlier version of this PR did some other things too, but I've removed those for now and will resubmit separately.)
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
#135639, though it's only a partial fix
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
The nftables mode of kube-proxy now uses less CPU when loading
very large rulesets.

commit.message:
Fix container_restart_test AfterEach failing when CRI Proxy is undefined

Two test contexts were failing because their AfterEach blocks run even
when BeforeEach skips the test (standard Ginkgo behavior). This caused
resetCRIProxyInjector to fail with "CRI Proxy is undefined".

Switched to using DeferCleanup inside BeforeEach instead, which only
runs if BeforeEach succeeds. This is the same pattern the other test
contexts in this file already use.

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

pull_requests.bodyText:
Two test contexts were failing because their AfterEach blocks run even when BeforeEach skips the test (standard Ginkgo behavior). This caused resetCRIProxyInjector to fail with "CRI Proxy is undefined".
Switched to using DeferCleanup inside BeforeEach instead, which only runs if BeforeEach succeeds. This is the same pattern the other test contexts in this file already use.

commit.message: feat(admissionregistration): wire group for declarative validation and require PolicyName

pull_requests.bodyText:
/kind feature
/sig api-machinery
/sig auth
What this PR does / why we need it

Enables the validation-gen framework for the admissionregistration.k8s.io API group (v1, v1beta1, v1alpha1).
Adds Declarative Validation (DV) support for ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding, focusing on spec.policyName.
Maintains parity with existing hand-written strategy validation as part of the incremental DV migration.
Marks the corresponding legacy validation logic for spec.policyName as covered by DV to avoid duplicate/partial coverage confusion during the transition.

Which issue(s) this PR is related to

Part of #134280
Part of #135752

Special notes for your reviewer

Scope is limited to enabling validation-gen + DV coverage for ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding.spec.policyName; no user-visible behavior changes intended.
Includes unit tests that verify parity between declarative and hand-written validations (create/update paths).
Follow-ups can extend DV coverage to additional ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding fields and related admissionregistration types.

Test plan

Table-driven unit tests covering:

Create & update validation for v1, v1beta1, and v1alpha1
Required field validation for spec.policyName
Parity checks via VerifyValidationEquivalence and VerifyUpdateValidationEquivalence



Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
NONE

nodes.bodyText:
Hi @AutuSnow. Thanks for your PR.
I'm waiting for a kubernetes member to verify that this patch is reasonable to test. If it is, they should reply with /ok-to-test on its own line. Until that is done, I will not automatically test new commits in this PR, but the usual testing commands by org members will still work. Regular contributors should join the org to skip this step.

This issue is currently awaiting triage.

The triage/accepted label can be added by org members by writing /triage accepted in a comment.
Details
Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available here.  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the kubernetes-sigs/prow repository.
/release-note-none
Why release note none? This caused significant pain for us.

@jsoref In the new version, the code for the tryReadVariableName function has not been modified, only the grpc version has been upgraded (v1.72.1 may have introduced stricter UTF-8 validation). I only reproduced this issue by adding unit testing. Whether this is the problem needs to be reviewed
/assign @thockin

@AutuSnow  Thanks for pointing out the issue! Quick question on a potential workaround:
We're running on EKS and the fix might take a while to reach us. In the meantime, can you confirm that escaping $ to $$ before non-ASCII characters would be a safe workaround?
For example, transforming $£FOO → $$£FOO in env var values before they hit K8s expansion.
My understanding is that $$ bypasses variable expansion entirely, so the buggy tryReadVariableName code path would never be reached. The value would then correctly expand to $£FOO at runtime.
Is this assumption correct?

@dhruvbehl In my opinion, your understanding is completely correct! Escaping $to $$is an effective temporary solution.
$$is recognized as an escape sequence
-In the tryReadVariableName function, when encountering $$, it will enter the case operator: branch
-Return a single $character, isVar = false
-Completely bypassed the problematic default: branch

Thanks!
/lgtm
/approve

commit.message: fix(expansion):Resolve the issue of UTF-8 characters being truncated, resulting in invalid UTF-8

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
What this PR does / why we need it:

The tryReadVariableName function uses string (input [0]) to only take the first byte, and multi byte UTF-8 characters are truncated, resulting in invalid UTF-8
The $+non ASCII characters in the environment variable value will be corrupted, resulting in invalid UTF-8 that will be rejected by the strict validation of gRPC v1.72+, causing container creation failure: grpc: error while marshalling: string field contains invalid UTF-8

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes:
#136323 (comment)
Fixes a 1.34 regression starting pods with environment variables with a value containing `$` followed by a multi-byte character

commit.message:
Add timeout to ImagePullTest poll loop to prevent infinite hangs

ImagePullTest was using wait.PollUntilContextCancel which has no
timeout, causing tests to hang for hours when the expected container
state is never reached (e.g., ErrImageNeverPull).

Changed to wait.PollUntilContextTimeout with ContainerStatusRetryTimeout
(5 minutes), matching the pattern used by other tests in the same file.

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

pull_requests.bodyText:

Hi @ngopalak-redhat. Thanks for your PR.

@bart0sh Please review and provide ok for test tag

@ngopalak-redhat Just wondering if this PR can introduce backward incompatibility. Some users may put their .conf files into subdirectories and this change would make Kubelet to ignore their configs. WDYT?

Are there any issues with including sub directories, besides not being idiomatic? I do also fear there are users relying on this behavior now, and dropping support for this behavior doesn't seem to simplify things much, so I'm leaning towards not doing it.

Are there any issues with including sub directories, besides not being idiomatic? I do also fear there are users relying on this behavior now, and dropping support for this behavior doesn't seem to simplify things much, so I'm leaning towards not doing it.

@haircommander Here's an issue with subdir:

If there are 2 files with same name one in the subdirectory and other in the top level directory, which one do you prioritize? For example "subdir/20test.conf" and "20test.conf". What's is the correct order?

(Sorry, I feel I responded to this day-before-yesterday. sorry if I had put my comment on a wrong pr)
I mean which is the kubelet currently doing (i.e. what does filepath.Walk do)? I think that's fine. we can write some tests to sanctify the behavior but I don't think we need special handling TBH

I mean which is the kubelet currently doing (i.e. what does filepath.Walk do)? I think that's fine. we can write some tests to sanctify the behavior but I don't think we need special handling TBH

@haircommander / @bart0sh I updated the PR to just add the test and document the current behavior. I'll squash the commit after getting an ok-for-test tag.

/ok-to-test
@ngopalak-redhat please, convert from draft to PR and squash commits if needed. CI jobs are not run for draft PRs as far as I remember.

/ok-to-test @ngopalak-redhat please, convert from draft to PR and squash commits if needed. CI jobs are not run for draft PRs as far as I remember.

commit.message:
pluginmanager: fix handling registration failures

Updated the pluginmanager to handle registration failures better
by removing the plugin from the actual state of the world and
deregistering when registration or notification fails.
Added a new e2e test to verify that the kubelet plugin manager retries
plugin registration when NotifyRegistrationStatus call fails.

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
What this PR does / why we need it:
Updated the pluginmanager to handle registration failures better by removing the plugin from the actual state of the world and deregistering when registration or notification fails. Added a new e2e test to verify that the kubelet plugin manager retries plugin registration when NotifyRegistrationStatus call fails.
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
N/A
Special notes for your reviewer:
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
The kubelet plugin manager now properly handles plugin registration failures by removing failed plugins from the actual state and retrying with exponential backoff (initial delay 500ms, doubling each failure up to ~2 minutes maximum) to protect against broken plugins causing denial of service while still allowing recovery from transient failures.

/sig node

commit.message: Improve logging, doc and test for kubelet config-dir file extension

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind cleanup

What this PR does / why we need it:
Problem
While using kubelet drop-in configuration files, we overlooked a critical requirement documented in the kubelet configuration guide:

The suffix of a valid kubelet drop-in configuration file must be .conf. For instance: 99-kubelet-address.conf

We created a drop-in file with a .yaml extension, and the configuration was silently ignored. Even with increased kubelet log verbosity, there was no indication that the file was being skipped, making this issue difficult to debug.
Changes
This PR improves the user experience when working with kubelet drop-in configurations:
1. Enhanced Logging

Added log statements at V(4) level to show which files are being skipped due to incorrect extensions
Logs include the filename and extension for better debugging
No impact on default logging (only visible with increased verbosity)

2. Improved Documentation

Updated the --config-dir flag documentation to explicitly mention the .conf extension requirement
Added example: 99-kubelet-address.conf
Clarified that only files with .conf extension will be loaded
This will update https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet/

3. Test Coverage

Added test case validating that .yaml files are ignored
Added test case validating that malformed YAML in .conf files causes proper errors

Testing
All existing tests pass. New test cases verify:

Files without .conf extension are ignored (addresses the reported issue)
Invalid YAML syntax in .conf files fails with clear error messages
Existing merge behavior remains unchanged

References

Documentation: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/kubelet-config-file/
Feature announcement: https://kubernetes.io/blog/2025/12/22/kubernetes-v1-35-kubelet-config-drop-in-directory-ga/

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

Special notes for your reviewer:
@haircommander We experienced this while testing memorySwap changes.
cc: @BhargaviGudi
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

NONE

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

commit.message:
Rename PodGroupInfo to PodGroupState

This is in preparation for PodGroupInfo struct with more pod group
details

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?

/kind cleanup
What this PR does / why we need it:
As mentioned in https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/5733/changes#r2610449539, we want to have a more focused PodGroupInfo type than the current implementation.
In this PR I rename PodGroupInfo to PodGroupState.
PodGroupState is used to track live pod state, which is currently used by the GangScheduling plugin.
The new PodGroupInfo type will describe a snapshotted state of a podgroup that will be used in the workload scheduling cycle plugins.
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

KEP: kubernetes/enhancements#5732
KEP: kubernetes/enhancements#4671
Special notes for your reviewer:
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

Renamed PodGroupInfo to PodGroupState, which can break custom scheduler plugins that use Handle.WorkloadManager

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

nodes.bodyText:
This PR may require API review.
If so, when the changes are ready, complete the pre-review checklist and request an API review.
Status of requested reviews is tracked in the API Review project.
/assign @saschagrunert @haircommander

Hello @yue9944882,
can you mention more on how you found this problem?
it would be helpful to know if this is a regression.

This may be related to PodResources KEP.
/cc @ndixita

It feels more related to InPlacePodVerticalScaling KEP.

  
    
      kubernetes/pkg/scheduler/framework/types.go
    
    
         Line 965
      in
      20114b2
    
  
  
    

        
          
           UseStatusResources: inPlacePodVerticalScalingEnabled, 
        
    
  


/cc @tallclair @natasha41575

@kannon92 we found this in an EKS customer cluster, the kube-scheduler kept loop-crashing due to this nil panic. so far the issue only applied to very few clusters so I think it only happens when certain (either correct or incorrect) pod content exists in the cluster.

What kind of pod triggers this failure? And are you testing InPlaceVertificalScaling?

It's a production cluster hence not testing anything. I don't have exact pod yaml information now because the cluster is deleted but the stack-traces are pretty straight-forward and self-explaining --- the nil panic can be happen when a pod with nil request map and non-nil request status present in the pod. whether it's anyone testing anything or not, the scheduler should be resilient to the api input as long as it's admitted by kube-apiserver.
Given this is a regression in 1.33, we will be backporting the fix, so this should be eligible to be included in 1.34.0 as well

/lgtm
/approve
/milestone v1.34
fix for a regression involving a panic / crash that is suitable for backporting

I am trying to find a way to reconfigure pods to stop hitting this problem and tried to repro this on a separate cluster but was not able to (when container has no resources defined and is an empty map or is not defined the cluster autoscaler works just fine, I do not have any other idea how to reproduce this.
We patched here a symptom, but I am afraid that we are still missing understanding of the rootcause.

We patched here a symptom, but I am afraid that we are still missing understanding of the rootcause.

We did identify the root cause of this panic in #132895 (comment)
It's possible there is a different bug with a similar symptom we need to root cause and fix. If you are seeing panics on current versions, can you open a new issue to track and provide what information you are able to try to help reproduce?

Just creating a pod with empty resources or not defining resources does not trigger this problem. I am interested in finding out whether there is any mitigation on the cluster/pod configuration level that would allow for fixing the issue.

commit.message:
fix(kubelet): convert V().Error() to V().Info() for verbosity-aware logging

The go-logr package ignores verbosity level (V()) for Error() calls,
causing these logs to always print regardless of the -v flag setting.
Convert to Info() with "err" key-value pair to respect verbosity levels.

This change covers 23 instances across kubelet components:
- prober (4 instances)
- lifecycle (4 instances)
- stats (3 instances)
- kuberuntime (2 instances)
- pleg (1 instance)
- allocation (1 instance)
- cm/devicemanager (1 instance)
- cm/dra (1 instance)
- metrics/collectors (1 instance)
- kubelet.go (3 instances)
- images/pullmanager (2 instances)

Ref: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/136027
Signed-off-by: thc1006 <84045975+thc1006@users.noreply.github.com>

fix(kubelet): convert V().Error() to V().Info() in volumemanager

Apply the same verbosity-aware logging fix to volumemanager components:
- desired_state_of_world.go (2 instances)
- reconciler/reconstruct.go (1 instance)

This completes the kubelet-wide fix for V().Error() ignoring verbosity.

Signed-off-by: thc1006 <84045975+thc1006@users.noreply.github.com>

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR fixes incorrect usage of logger.V(N).Error() across the kubelet package. The go-logr package design causes Error() calls to bypass verbosity level checks entirely, meaning these logs are always printed regardless of the configured verbosity level.
This causes production log flooding and defeats the purpose of using verbosity levels.
Which issue(s) this PR fixes:
Fixes #136027
Special notes for your reviewer:
The linter check from kubernetes-sigs/logtools#32 identified 21 instances across the kubelet package. This PR addresses all of them.
Files modified (15 files, 21 instances):

pkg/kubelet/allocation/allocation_manager.go
pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/plugin/v1beta1/client.go
pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/plugin/dra_plugin_manager.go
pkg/kubelet/images/pullmanager/metrics.go
pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go
pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_container_linux.go
pkg/kubelet/lifecycle/handlers.go
pkg/kubelet/metrics/collectors/cri_metrics.go
pkg/kubelet/pleg/generic.go
pkg/kubelet/prober/prober.go
pkg/kubelet/prober/prober_manager.go
pkg/kubelet/stats/cri_stats_provider.go
pkg/kubelet/stats/helper.go
pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/cache/desired_state_of_world.go
pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/reconciler/reconstruct.go

Conversion patterns applied:

V(N).Error(nil, "msg", ...) → V(N).Info("msg", ...)
V(N).Error(err, "msg", ...) → V(N).Info("msg", ..., "err", err)

Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
Fixed kubelet logging to properly respect verbosity levels. Previously, some debug/info messages using V().Error() would always be printed regardless of the configured log verbosity.

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Alarm Proposal Docs), usage docs, etc.:
N/A
/sig node
/area kubelet

What type of PR is this?
/kind flake
What this PR does / why we need it:
After #135393, the scheduler may retry after a PreBind rejection, causing PreBind and Unreserve to be invoked more than once.
This PR introduces a PreEnqueue gate which blocks the pod from being re-enqueued after a PreBind attempt has been made, ensuring that PreBind and Unreserve are called exactly once per test case.

commit.message:
Fix volume/nfs image for CentOS Stream 9

The volume/nfs:1.5 image was updated from CentOS 8 to CentOS Stream 9
in commit 9b47a096f4b, but this broke NFS server startup because
NFSv2 is no longer supported in RHEL 9 / CentOS Stream 9.

The `-N 2` flag (disable NFSv2) passed to rpc.mountd and rpc.nfsd
causes them to fail with "2: Unsupported version" since there's no
NFSv2 to disable. This prevented rpc.nfsd from starting, causing
NFS mount timeouts in e2e tests.

Fix by removing the `-N 2` flags. NFSv2 is already unavailable in
CentOS Stream 9, so explicitly disabling it is unnecessary.

Red Hat documentation states: "NFS version 2 (NFSv2) is no longer
supported by Red Hat."

Reference: https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html/managing_file_systems/mounting-nfs-shares_managing-file-systems

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

Fix by removing the -N 2 flags. NFSv2 is already unavailable in CentOS Stream 9, so explicitly disabling it is unnecessary.
Red Hat documentation states: "NFS version 2 (NFSv2) is no longer supported by Red Hat."

commit.message:
Remove ppc64le and s390x support from npb-is image

The npb-is image was failing to build on ppc64le with relocation overflow:
  relocation truncated to fit: R_PPC64_TOC16_HA against symbol 'key_array'

Remove ppc64le and s390x from supported architectures:
- The IS benchmark with CLASS=D has ~8GB data arrays that require special
  compiler flags for non-x86 architectures
- ppc64le and s390x builds are difficult to test and maintain
- Keep amd64 and arm64 which are the primary node performance test targets

I don't think these arch(es) ever worked!

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message:
webhook admission: avoid risk of flakes

The unit test flaked at least once so far in the CI. Cannot be reproduced
locally, though.

    === RUN   TestGetMutatingWebhookConfigSmartReload/create_configuration_and_update_moar_of_them
        mutating_webhook_manager_test.go:238: Expected number of webhooks 4 received 0
        mutating_webhook_manager_test.go:245: Expected number of creations 4 received 0
        mutating_webhook_manager_test.go:266: Expected final number of webhooks 5 received 3
    --- FAIL: TestGetMutatingWebhookConfigSmartReload/create_configuration_and_update_moar_of_them (2.00s)

There were two potential root causes:
- The watch must be set up completely before creating objects (a fake
  client-go limitation, fix separately).
- The delay might be too small on a loaded system.

By running the tests in a synctest bubble with synctest.Wait calls at
the right places both problems are avoided. As a welcome side effect
the test also completes faster.

Before:

    ok  	k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/configuration	21.036s

After:

    ok  	k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission/configuration	4.029s

    2m0s: 13801 runs so far, 0 failures

commit.message: Switch ipvs and winkernel back to more regular forced syncs

pull_requests.bodyText:
What this PR does / why we need it:
In the BoundedFrequencyRunner rewrite (#131615) we changed the "resync even if nothing changed" period for ipvs and winkernel from 30s (configurable) to 1h (hardcoded). (It was already 1h for iptables and nftables.) This just flips it back.
In most cases the extra resyncs are unnecessary and wasteful, but maybe some people have use cases where they need them, so let's not change the behavior right before deprecating IPVS.
(@princepereira The same change happened for winkernel and I'm reverting that back to the 1.33 behavior now too.)
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes #134147
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
Fixes a 1.34+ regression in ipvs and winkernel kube-proxy backends; these are now reverted back to their
pre-1.34 behavior of regularly rechecking all of their rules even when no
Services or EndpointSlices change.

/kind bug
/kind regression
/sig network
/sig windows
/area kube-proxy
/area ipvs
/priority important-soon

commit.message:
Switch node perf tests from TensorFlow to PyTorch Wide-Deep

Replace the TensorFlow-based wide-deep workload with the PyTorch
implementation. This change:

- Adds pytorchWideDeepWorkload using the new pytorch-wide-deep image (1.0.0)
- Removes tfWideDeepWorkload and tf-wide-deep image references
- Enables arm64 support (PyTorch image is multi-arch)
- Uses the same log parsing (time -p output format)

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message:
Add integration test for MutablePodResourcesForSuspendedJobs with PodReplacementPolicy=Failed

This test verifies that when a job with PodReplacementPolicy=Failed is
suspended and pods are terminating, resource updates can be made to the
job but new pods are only created after terminating pods are removed.
The new pods should have the updated resources.

commit.message:
Fix goroutine leak in pluginmanager test

Use a buffered channel for the operation start signal to prevent
goroutines from blocking indefinitely. When the test receiver times
out before all goroutines send their signal, an unbuffered channel
causes those goroutines to block forever on the send operation.
The buffer size is computed dynamically using max() to ensure it
always accommodates the maximum number of concurrent operations
any test may spawn.

commit.message:
Fix post-kubernetes-push-e2e-node-perf-npb-is-test-images problem (arm64)

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

pull_requests.bodyText:
Trying to fix:
https://storage.googleapis.com/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/post-kubernetes-push-e2e-node-perf-npb-is-test-images/2014181091459469312/build-log.txt
The arm64 build of npb-is was failing with relocation overflow errors because the IS benchmark with CLASS=D produces large binaries that exceed the default code model limits.
Add arm64-specific compiler flags to fix the relocation overflow:

-mcmodel=large: use large code model for > 4GB address space
-fno-PIE: disable position-independent executable (incompatible with large model)
-mno-outline-atomics: inline atomics to avoid linker issues

tests succeed.

Gingko progress reports when a test is stuck).

pull_requests.title: Enhance content negotiation for zpages

nodes.bodyText: I think this needs a changelog entry. If not, what's the thinking behind not including one?
If CBOR is enabled, can you fetch z pages as CBOR?

/triage accepted
/assign

I think this needs a changelog entry. If not, what's the thinking behind not including one?

Added it, thanks for noticing.

This prevents erroneous 500 http code returned earlier for Accept: application/yaml;v=v1alpha1;g=config.k8s.io;as=Statusz

unsupported requested type should return a 406 error, not a 5xx, by fixing the default case of our switch statement:
		switch serializerInfo.MediaType {
...
		default:

I'm open to accepting application/json, application/yaml, and application/cbor if it's as simple as expanding our switch statement here (I think all three serializers should be able to serialize resulting object):
		switch serializerInfo.MediaType {
		case "application/json","application/yaml","application/cbor":

Also, I noticed another thing, the error message returned for an unsupported media was the following:
root@zpages-test-control-plane:/# curl --cacert /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt   --cert /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.crt  --key /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.key -H "Accept: application/foo;v=v1alpha1;g=config.k8s.io;as=Statusz" https://localhost:6443/statusz
{
  "kind": "Status",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "metadata": {},
  "status": "Failure",
  "message": "only the following media types are accepted: application/json, application/yaml, application/vnd.kubernetes.protobuf, text/plain, application/cbor",
  "reason": "NotAcceptable",
  "code": 406
}

The error included application/vnd.kubernetes.protobuf as a supported media type which is incorrect. I have tried to fix this by adding a wrapper around CodecFactory (statuszCodecFactory & flagzCodecFactory), and exclude protobuf from this wrapper's SupportedMediaTypes() implementation. With this, the error now shows:
curl --cacert /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt   --cert /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.crt  --key /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.key -H "Accept: application/foo;v=v1alpha1;g=config.k8s.io;as=Statusz" https://localhost:6443/statusz
{
  "kind": "Status",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "metadata": {},
  "status": "Failure",
  "message": "only the following media types are accepted: application/json, application/yaml, text/plain, application/cbor",
  "reason": "NotAcceptable",
  "code": 406
}

Happy to also revise this if this is not the preferred approach to fix the wrong list of supported types in the error.

/lgtm
/approve
LGTM label has been added.  DetailsGit tree hash: 859e15a9b6eef56b933d811f9723789475f9687c

commit.message: Fix TestTerminationOrderingSidecarStopAfterMain flake under stress

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?

What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR fixes the TestTerminationOrderingSidecarStopAfterMain test flake.
To summarize as a part of test, there are 2 go routines are being created one for main container and another for sidecar and there is sleep of 1 sec in the main container go routine.
The expectation is that the side car go routine scheduled first and the timer is started, by the time main container go routines is finished the side car should record 1 sec time.
The hidden assumption here is that the sidecar go routine scheduled first which may not be the case as the main go routine can be scheduled when the system under load which may lead to flakes with below error message
    kuberuntime_termination_order_test.go:75: expected sidecar to wait for main container to exit, got delay of 0

Which issue(s) this PR is related to:

commit.message:
Fix NPB test images arm64 cross-build failures

The NPB (NAS Parallel Benchmarks) test images were failing to build
for arm64 architecture due to missing shebangs in NPB shell scripts.

The sys/print_header and sys/print_instructions scripts in NPB lack
default /bin/sh), they fail with "Error 127" (command not found) during
QEMU-emulated cross-builds.

Fix by adding shebangs to these scripts during the Docker build.

Changes:
- npb-ep: Add shebang fix, bump version 1.5 → 1.6.0
- npb-is: Add shebang fix, bump version 1.4 → 1.5.0

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message:
kubelet: migrate kubelet_getters.go to contextual logging

  Migrate kubelet_getters.go and related functions to use contextual logging.
  Functions that only need logging receive logger klog.Logger directly instead
  of ctx context.Context.

  Changes:
  - getPodVolumePathListFromDisk: ctx -> logger
  - getMountedVolumePathListFromDisk: ctx -> logger
  - getPodVolumeSubpathListFromDisk: ctx -> logger
  - getLastObservedNodeAddresses: ctx -> logger
  - podVolumesExist: ctx -> logger
  - removeOrphanedPodVolumeDirs: ctx -> logger
  - cleanupOrphanedPodDirs: ctx -> logger
  - cleanupOrphanedPodCgroups: ctx -> logger

  Callers use klog.FromContext(ctx) to obtain logger from existing context.

commit.message: feat: use contextual HandleCrash when possible

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind cleanup
What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR is piece of #135524 that was to big an covered many different SIG, this PR focus on call to utilruntime.HandleError under /pkg/kubelet.
When possible, updates existing calls to the non-contextual API utilruntime.HandleError within the new context-aware version utilruntime.HandleErrorWithContext or utilruntime.HandleErrorWithLogger.
This is required as part of the Kubernetes structured logging initiative to ensure that log output from this component is structured, carries contextual fields, and correctly respects context cancellation signals.
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Related to #126379
Special notes for your reviewer:
Context Prioritization:
The inherited context.Context from the parent function was consistently reused and passed to the updated HandleErrorWithContext calls.
Where a context was absent but a logger was available, the call was updated to HandleErrorWithLogger.
Available context or logger instances stored as attributes of the implementing struct were used for the contextual calls (WithContext or WithLogger).
The context was extracted from any passed-in HTTP request or WebSocket object and used to complete the call to HandleErrorWithContext.
/wg structured-logging

NONE

commit.message:
Update OpenTelemetry dependencies to latest versions

Core packages (opentelemetry-go):
- go.opentelemetry.io/otel: v1.38.0 → v1.39.0
- go.opentelemetry.io/otel/metric: v1.38.0 → v1.39.0
- go.opentelemetry.io/otel/trace: v1.38.0 → v1.39.0
- go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk: v1.38.0 → v1.39.0

Exporters:
- go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace: v1.34.0 → v1.39.0
- go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace/otlptracegrpc: v1.34.0 → v1.39.0

Contrib instrumentation (opentelemetry-go-contrib):
- go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/.../otelhttp: v0.61.0 → v0.64.0
- go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/.../otelrestful: v0.44.0 → v0.64.0

Protocol definitions (opentelemetry-proto-go):
- go.opentelemetry.io/proto/otlp: v1.5.0 → v1.9.0

Notable changes:
- Go 1.24 is now the minimum required version (Go 1.23 support dropped) for OTEL components
- Performance: ~4x improvement in histogram concurrent operations; xxhash
  replaces fnv for attribute hashing
- Fixed goroutine leak in span processors when context is canceled
- otelrestful migrated semantic conventions from v1.20.0 to v1.34.0
  (e.g., http.method → http.request.method)
- Partial OTLP export errors now surfaced instead of being silently dropped
- otelrestful no longer depends on json-iterator/go, modern-go/concurrent,
  or modern-go/reflect2; unwanted-dependencies.json updated accordingly

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message: Ensure that processing does not block queue writers
Add benchmarks for adding objects with slow handlers

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind feature

What this PR does / why we need it:
Pulls apart processing from taking the FIFO lock in order to allow other writers to write to the queue while controller processing happens. This allows for watches to stay alive even with lots of contention on the underlying store.
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
KEP: kubernetes/enhancements#5647

Special notes for your reviewer:
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

client-go informers can now enqueue new watch events while already-queued events are being processed. This avoids dropping watches during a burst of incoming events due to contention on slow processing. This behavior is controlled by the UnlockWhileProcessing client-go feature gate, which is enabled by default.

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

commit.message: Fix(kubelet/test): flaky TestWaitForAllPodsUnmount by adding goroutine synchronization

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
﻿
/kind flake
/kind cleanup
﻿
What this PR does / why we need it:
﻿
Fixes flaky TestWaitForAllPodsUnmount that was failing intermittently due to race conditions in test goroutine synchronization.
﻿
Problem:
The test was unstable because volume simulation goroutines were started without ensuring they were ready before calling WaitForAttachAndMount. This caused timing-dependent failures where:

Goroutines might not start before attach/mount verification began
﻿
Solution:


Added channel-based synchronization to ensure all simulateVolumeInUseUpdate goroutines signal readiness before proceeding
﻿
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
﻿
Fixes #
#136255

Testing:

✅ Single run: PASS (35-40s)
✅ 20 sequential runs: 100% pass rate
✅ 10-minute stress test (4 parallel): 120+ runs, 0 failures
✅ Race detector stress (2 parallel): 50+ runs, 0 failures, 0 race warnings
﻿
Stress test command used:

timeout 10m stress -p 4 go test -run TestWaitForAllPodsUnmount -timeout=5m ./pkg/kubelet/volumemanager/

commit.message:
Fix sample-apiserver image build failure

The kube-cross image has GOPATH=/usr/local/go and GOROOT=/usr/local/go
(same directory). When `go install .` runs, the binary is placed in
/usr/local/go/bin/, but the Makefile was searching for it in /go/bin/
which is empty.

Fix by using `go build -o` to place the binary directly at the target
location instead of relying on go install + find.

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message: Fix for preferred dualstack and required dualstack in winkernel proxier.

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR fixes dual-stack service handling in the Windows (winkernel) kube-proxy implementation by correctly separating IPv4 and IPv6 load balancer state.
Specifically, it updates the Windows proxier to:

Correctly identify whether a Service VIP and its endpoints are IPv4 or IPv6
Include an explicit isIPv6 dimension in the load balancer identifier, ensuring IPv4 and IPv6 load balancers are tracked independently
Prevent incorrect reuse or collision of HNS load balancer policies between IPv4 and IPv6 services
Properly support both PreferDualStack and RequireDualStack Service types during sync and reconciliation. The primary change in this PR is that, while the BaseServicePortInfo object already maintains IP-family–filtered lists of externalIPs and loadBalancerVIPs derived from service.Spec.ExternalIPs and service.Status.LoadBalancer.Ingress, the WinKernel service info previously re-extracted these values directly from the Service object without applying IP-family filtering. This led to incorrect behavior for dual-stack services. The updated implementation eliminates this duplication by sourcing the required information directly from BaseServicePortInfo instead of reprocessing the Service spec and status.

Without this change, the Windows kube-proxy may incorrectly mix IPv4 and IPv6 backends, fail to create required IPv6 load balancers, or reuse an existing IPv4 load balancer for IPv6 traffic. This results in broken or incomplete dual-stack behavior on Windows nodes.
By explicitly separating load balancer state per IP family, this PR enables correct, predictable dual-stack service programming on Windows, bringing winkernel behavior closer to Linux kube-proxy and unblocking dual-stack AKS / Windows scenarios.
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes #136240
KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-network/563-dual-stack
Special notes for your reviewer:
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

Fixed an issue in the Windows kube-proxy (winkernel) where IPv4 and IPv6 Service load balancers could be incorrectly shared, causing broken dual-stack Service behavior. The kube-proxy now tracks load balancers per IP family, enabling correct support for PreferDualStack and RequireDualStack Services on Windows nodes.

commit.message:
test/images: add glibc-dns-testing image (replaces jessie-dnsutils)

The jessie-dnsutils image build has been failing since Debian Jessie reached
end-of-life (EOL) and its repositories were moved to archive.debian.org:

  W: Failed to fetch http://deb.debian.org/debian/dists/jessie/Release
     404  Not Found [IP: 151.101.86.132 80]
  E: Some index files failed to download.

This adds a new glibc-dns-testing image to replace jessie-dnsutils. The new
name better reflects the image's purpose: testing glibc DNS resolution behavior
(vs. musl in Alpine-based agnhost).

The name glibc-dns-testing was recommended by maintainers to reflect that
the image's purpose is testing glibc-based DNS resolution, which behaves
differently from musl libc (used by Alpine/agnhost). Key differences include:
- glibc queries nameservers sequentially; musl queries in parallel
- glibc and musl handle ndots and search domains differently
- The hostname command behaves differently between glibc and musl

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message:
Add client-go credential plugin to kuberc

Remove reference to internal types in kuberc types

* Remove unserialized types from public APIs

Also remove defaulting

* Don't do conversion gen for plugin policy types

Because the plugin policy types are explicitly allowed to be empty, they
should not affect conversion. The autogenerated conversion functions for
the `Preference` type will leave those fields empty.

* Remove defaulting tests

Comments and simplifications (h/t jordan liggitt)

Signed-off-by: Peter Engelbert <pmengelbert@gmail.com>

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind feature
What this PR does / why we need it:
It permits the user to specify a client-go credential plugin allowlist as a security knob. The user may specify it in kuberc. See design details in KEP-3104
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
KEP: kubernetes/enhancements#3104
Special notes for your reviewer:
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
yes.

Changed kuberc configuration schema. Two new optional fields added to kuberc configuration, `credPluginPolicy` and `credPluginAllowlist`. This is documented in [KEP-3104](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-cli/3104-introduce-kuberc/README.md#allowlist-design-details) and documentation is added to the website by [kubernetes/website#52877](https://github.com/kubernetes/website/pull/52877)

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:

- [KEP]: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/3104
- [Usage]: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-cli/3104-introduce-kuberc/README.md#allowlist-design-details

commit.message:
test/images: Fix zookeeper-installer netcat package for bookworm

In Debian bookworm, 'netcat' is a virtual package with no installation
candidate. Replace it with 'netcat-openbsd' which provides the actual
netcat implementation.

Build failure:
```
 > [2/7] RUN clean-install wget bash netcat:
0.382 Get:6 http://deb.debian.org/debian-security bookworm-security/main amd64 Packages [291 kB]

3.174 Reading state information...
3.180 Package netcat is a virtual package provided by:
3.180   netcat-openbsd 1.219-1
3.180   netcat-traditional 1.10-47
3.180
3.182 E: Package 'netcat' has no installation candidate
------
```

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message:
test/images: Update npb-ep and npb-is base images to bookworm-slim

Debian stretch and buster have reached end of life and their
package repositories are no longer available. Update the NAS
Parallel Benchmarks images to use Debian bookworm-slim:

- npb-is: stretch-slim -> bookworm-slim (version 1.2 -> 1.3)
- npb-ep: buster-slim -> bookworm-slim (version 1.3 -> 1.4)

For npb-ep, also remove the hardcoded buster apt sources and
version-pinned packages that are no longer needed with bookworm.

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message:
test/images: fix tf-wide-deep build by using models v1.9.0

The wide_deep directory was removed from tensorflow/models after v1.9.0.
Commit f8ca585b666 updated models from v1.9.0 to v2.15.0, breaking the
build because /models-2.15.0/official/wide_deep/data_download.py no
longer exists.

Revert to models v1.9.0 which contains the wide_deep implementation.
The v1.9.0 code uses tf.estimator APIs which are still available in
TensorFlow 2.x.

Fixes: https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/post-kubernetes-push-e2e-node-perf-tf-wide-deep-test-images/2011957382287986688

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

pull_requests.bodyText:
The wide_deep directory was removed from tensorflow/models after v1.9.0. Commit f8ca585 updated models from v1.9.0 to v2.15.0, breaking the build because /models-2.15.0/official/wide_deep/data_download.py no longer exists.
Revert to models v1.9.0 which contains the wide_deep implementation. The v1.9.0 code uses tf.estimator APIs which are still available in TensorFlow 2.x.
See broken run logs at:
https://prow.k8s.io/view/gs/kubernetes-ci-logs/logs/post-kubernetes-push-e2e-node-perf-tf-wide-deep-test-images/2011957382287986688

nodes.bodyText:

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available here.  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the kubernetes-sigs/prow repository.
/hold

cc @dlipovetsky
/assign

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is APPROVED
This pull-request has been approved by: pacoxu, SataQiu
The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found here.
The pull request process is described here
Details
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files:

cmd/kubeadm/OWNERS [SataQiu,pacoxu]

Approvers can indicate their approval by writing /approve in a comment
Approvers can cancel approval by writing /approve cancel in a comment

kubeadm: verify the etcd static pod is running before promoting it from a learner member to be a voting member. If the etcd pod failed to start, the added learner member will be removed from the cluster automatically.

CreateStackedEtcdStaticPodManifestFile is only called on kubeadm join actually.
maybe clarify it as ...the cluster automatically on 'kubeadm join'.
EDIT: or start the release note with kubeadm: during 'kubeadm join' ....

this change adds another timeout in between ControlPlaneComponentHealthCheck, but there is no guarantee if the same users will hit this timeout first. for these big DBs they must configure the timeouts. kubeadm will not block forever, obviously.
the change overall seems OK to me, but kubeadm has a history of breaking something related to the etcd logic, when trying to improve something in the etcd logic.

Both good points, @neolit123.
CAPI only recently exposed the timeouts in the Kubeadm Bootstrap Provider API, and backporting could impact users that cannot easily increase the timeout.
On the other hand, can we increase the default timeout (from its value of 2 minutes) in this PR, to account for the new wait we introduce here?
(If we increase the default timeout, will that address your concerns about backporting?)

this change adds another timeout in between ControlPlaneComponentHealthCheck, but there is no guarantee if the same users will hit this timeout first. for these big DBs they must configure the timeouts. kubeadm will not block forever, obviously.
the change overall seems OK to me, but kubeadm has a history of breaking something related to the etcd logic, when trying to improve something in the etcd logic.

Both good points, @neolit123.
CAPI only recently exposed the timeouts in the Kubeadm Bootstrap Provider API, and backporting could impact users that cannot easily increase the timeout.

CAPI adopted the timeouts struct long after the v1beta4 support was added.
maybe a maintainer BW issue.

On the other hand, can we increase the default timeout (from its value of 2 minutes) in this PR, to account for the new wait we introduce here?
(If we increase the default timeout, will that address your concerns about backporting?)

i think if there is timeout increase it should be a separate PR. with some of the contention around etcd member remove we might not backport this PR.

I'm sorry to be making this comment so late in the review cycle, but I think "etcd pod is running" is not sufficient to promote the etcd learner. That is because, although the etcd pod is "running," it in fact may be crash-looping:

At least one container is still running, or is in the process of starting or restarting.
-- https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/pod-lifecycle/#pod-phase

I still think "etcd pod is running" a necessary condition to wait for, and it is very useful to report when the condition is not met. However, I think kubeadm should also call the etcd member API and verify that the new member is "started," before trying to promote the member.

I'm sorry to be making this comment so late in the review cycle, but I think "etcd pod is running" is not sufficient to promote the etcd learner. That is because, although the etcd pod is "running," it in fact may be crash-looping:

At least one container is still running, or is in the process of starting or restarting.
-- https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/pod-lifecycle/#pod-phase

I still think "etcd pod is running" a necessary condition to wait for, and it is very useful to report when the condition is not met. However, I think kubeadm should also call the etcd member API and verify that the new member is "started," before trying to promote the member.

these are all topics that we could have discussed on the ticket.
instead of waiting for a container to start, what about waiting for the member to appear in the member list?
that would mean:

add member
start the pod
retry with timeout of etcd API call until the member appears
retry with timeout of etcd API call to promote it

this idea removes the need to wait for pod  start and container start

@pacoxu I see you already approved this PR, but I think we should consider the other approach we started discussing above. Let's discuss on the issue, starting with kubernetes/kubeadm#3269 (comment), so we do not lose the discussion if we create a new PR.
/retest

Thank you for the new implementation, @SataQiu!
Thanks for tagging me, @neolit123. I left a couple comments about the implementation.

If I have understood it correctly, once my comment is addressed, e.g. using the code suggested by @neolit123), then this PR does not change how kubeadm behaves, e.g. no timeouts are changed. The PR simply improves the information returned to the user in case something goes wrong with the local etcd member.
If that is true, I think we might consider backporting this PR.
/lgtm
/lgtm

#136014 (comment)
@HirazawaUi WDYT, about the above assessment?
LGTM label has been added.  DetailsGit tree hash: 09c55e2b2c63cf5afb83500e32a7b9404e3c38de
/retest

/lgtm
/hold cancel
/retest

commit.message:
Update gRPC ecosystem dependencies

Update the gRPC ecosystem to pick up performance improvements,
bug fixes, and maintain compatibility with the latest protobuf
and OpenTelemetry releases.

Notable changes in grpc v1.78.0:
- mem.Reader interface changed to struct
- Legacy pick_first load balancer policy removed (pickfirstleaf)
- Improved connection state management

Updated dependencies:
- grpc-gateway/v2: v2.27.4 (2025-12-26)
- go-grpc-middleware/v2: v2.3.3 (2025-11-04)
- go-grpc-middleware/providers/prometheus: v1.1.0 (2025-06-16)
- google.golang.org/grpc: v1.78.0 (2025-12-23)
- genproto/googleapis/api: v0.0.0-20260112192933-99fd39fd28a9 (2026-01-12)
- genproto/googleapis/rpc: v0.0.0-20260112192933-99fd39fd28a9 (2026-01-12)

Signed-off-by: Davanum Srinivas <davanum@gmail.com>

commit.message:
Concatenate string instead of using fmt.Sprintf

The call to `fmt.Sprintf` does virtually the same as concatenating the
strings, but incurs in minor overhead from having to call a function,
and performs an additional allocation.

I've made a small benchmark for this and when run against current
`master` and this branch, the results are significant for this
function:

    goos: darwin
    goarch: arm64
    pkg: k8s.io/client-go/transport
    cpu: Apple M1 Pro
                              │ bart.base.log │           bart.concat.log            │
                              │    sec/op     │    sec/op     vs base                │
    BearerAuthRoundTripper-10    361.3n ± 31%   295.5n ± 11%  -18.21% (p=0.000 n=20)

                              │ bart.base.log │          bart.concat.log          │
                              │     B/op      │    B/op     vs base               │
    BearerAuthRoundTripper-10      768.0 ± 0%   752.0 ± 0%  -2.08% (p=0.000 n=20)

                              │ bart.base.log │          bart.concat.log           │
                              │   allocs/op   │ allocs/op   vs base                │
    BearerAuthRoundTripper-10      6.000 ± 0%   5.000 ± 0%  -16.67% (p=0.000 n=20)

Considering this method is likely used in many installations, the
gains, while small, adds up to bigger savings

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind feature
What this PR does / why we need it:
In the bearerAuthRoundTripper used in the client, every call to RoundTrip will add the Authorization: Bearer %s header to each request. The value of this header was built using fmt.Sprintf, doing virtually the same as concatenating strings; using fmt.Sprintf for this simple operation incurs in minor overhead from having to call a function, and performs an additional allocation.
I've made a small benchmark for this and when run against current master and this branch, the results are significant for this function:
goos: darwin
goarch: arm64
pkg: k8s.io/client-go/transport
cpu: Apple M1 Pro
                          │ bart.base.log │           bart.concat.log            │
                          │    sec/op     │    sec/op     vs base                │
BearerAuthRoundTripper-10    361.3n ± 31%   295.5n ± 11%  -18.21% (p=0.000 n=20)

                          │ bart.base.log │          bart.concat.log          │
                          │     B/op      │    B/op     vs base               │
BearerAuthRoundTripper-10      768.0 ± 0%   752.0 ± 0%  -2.08% (p=0.000 n=20)

                          │ bart.base.log │          bart.concat.log           │
                          │   allocs/op   │ allocs/op   vs base                │
BearerAuthRoundTripper-10      6.000 ± 0%   5.000 ± 0%  -16.67% (p=0.000 n=20)

Considering this method is likely used in many installations, the gains, while small, adds up to bigger savings.
Which issue(s) this PR fixes:

Special notes for your reviewer:
Note that I mention a benchmark, this wasn't include in my changes because I couldn't find a similar one in the codebase, but I can add it in this PR. The benchmark is very simple:
func BenchmarkBearerAuthRoundTripper(b *testing.B) {
	rt := &testRoundTripper{}
	req := &http.Request{}
	bart := NewBearerAuthRoundTripper("test", rt)
	b.ReportAllocs()
	for b.Loop() {
		bart.RoundTrip(req)
	}
}
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

NONE

commit.message: kubelet(dra): fix multiple claims handling
test(e2e/dra): add test for pod requesting allocated and new claims
test(ut/dra): add unit test for pod requesting prepared and new claims

pull_requests.bodyText:
What type of PR is this?
/kind bug
What this PR does / why we need it:
This PR fixes an issue in the kubelet DRA (Dynamic Resource Allocation) manager where preparing multiple ResourceClaims fails if one of the claims is already prepared.
Previously, if a claim was already prepared, the loop would exit early, causing subsequent claims not to be prepared.
Now, the kubelet continues preparing remaining claims even if some claims are already prepared.
Which issue(s) this PR is related to:
Fixes #135901
Special notes for your reviewer:
None
Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
kubelet(dra): correctly handles multiple ResourceClaims even if one is already prepared

Additional documentation e.g., KEPs (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals), usage docs, etc.:
